elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the stability of scalar perturbations around a magnetized stationary compact object in General Relativity.
elements.sent-2411-002: The considered object is one of the simplest exact solutions of Einstein electrovacuum equations corresponding to a spheroidal body endowed with a dipole magnetic moment.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is effectively constructed by imposing a perfect reflection (mirror) boundary condition on a central region of the Gutsunaev-Manko spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-004: A time-domain analysis of the perturbations reveals a quasinormal phase followed by a power-law decaying tail.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our findings suggest that the exterior region of the magnetized compact object is stable in the entire parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, the system tends to become generically more stable the stronger the magnetization of the central object is.
elements.sent-2411-007: Such findings can be useful for the qualitative understanding of more realistic astrophysical situations involving highly magnetized sources.
elements.sent-2411-008: Einstein field equations display an extraordinary diversity of solutions with different and exciting properties.
elements.sent-2411-009: The Schwarzschild metric {is the simplest possible solution describing an astrophysical object, and many of its generalizations have astrophysical relevance \cite{ PhysRev.56.455, 1642270, stephani2009exact .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: From an observational point of view, its most reasonable stationary generalizations should include rotation and magnetization since these are typical physical attributes of astrophysical sources.
elements.sent-2411-011: We are mainly concerned here with the case of magnetized axisymmetric non-rotating bodies as described by the exterior region of the Gutsunaev-Manko spacetime \cite{gutsunaev1987gravitational,gutsunaev1988family,manko1989new,manko1989 , which can be asymptotically interpreted as a spherically symmetric core endowed with a dipole magnetic moment.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Such a solution is certainly of astrophysical interest since it provides a simple yet viable description of the exterior region of a magnetar, {\it i.e. , a highly magnetized neutron star, typically with low angular momentum \cite{Kaspi_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This class of solutions has been gaining prominence in the recent literature \cite{deFreitas:2017rbk, Polanco:2023jsp .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The intense magnetic fields (of the order of $10^9$ to $10^{11}$ T) are prone to produce high-energy electromagnetic radiation phenomena {such as bursts and flares of X-rays, gamma-rays, and perhaps even Fast Radio Bursts (FRB) \cite{Kirsten_2020, zhang2020physical .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: This possibility has gained interest due to the recently reported detection \cite{ChimeFrb_1 of a repeating FRB from the magnetar SGR 1935+2154, which lies in the outskirts of our own Milky Way.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The investigation of these phenomena is one of the main scientific goals of the soon-to-start operating BINGO radio telescope \cite{Abdalla_2022, santos2023bingo .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Previous enterprises concerning instability of localized solutions turn out to provide hints into modifications of interactions \cite{abdalla2019instability or even traditional solutions with chosen specific perturbations \cite{Zhu:2014sya .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The exterior region of a magnetar is expected to be well described by an asymptotically flat electrovacuum spacetime with a magnetic field originating in the strong magnetization of the central core.
elements.sent-2411-019: This is precisely the situation corresponding to the Gutsunaev-Manko spacetime, whose stability we analyze here.
elements.sent-2411-020: It is also possible to introduce a low angular momentum into this picture through an approximate solution, see \cite{morachaverri2024classical .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, we {opt to consider only the non-rotating case and explore the effects of magnetization on the stability of the exterior region of these compact objects.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Hence, the exterior region of our compact object is, by construction, a regular asymptotically flat magnetized spacetime, and we will consider its stability by exploring a quasinormal mode (QNM) \cite{kokkotas1999quasi,konoplya2011quasinormal,abdalla2019instability analysis of scalar perturbations with a total reflection (mirror) boundary condition on the surface of the central core.}
elements.sent-2411-023: On physical grounds, one expects that such QNMs are stable, and we found that they indeed are.
elements.sent-2411-024: But, interestingly, we found that the presence of magnetization improves the stability of the spacetime, {\it i.e. , the larger the magnetization, the larger the perturbations exponential suppression, and consequently the faster the relaxation of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-026: In Section 2 we will briefly review the construction of our compact object spacetime starting from the Gutsunaev-Manko solution.
elements.sent-2411-027: Section 3 will be devoted to the numerical scheme for the {time-domain calculation of the scalar perturbation.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Features such as tails and quasi-normal modes are considered.
elements.sent-2411-029: The limit of small magnetization will be compared with an analytical calculation in the Schwarzschild limit.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, in Section 4 we will discuss our results and some of its astrophysical implications, and suggest possible future directions for these studies.
elements.sent-2411-031: The objects considered in this paper are assumed to be modeled by the simplest exact solutions of Einstein electrovacuum equations corresponding to a prolate spheroidal body endowed with a dipole magnetic moment.
elements.sent-2411-032: Effectively, they are constructed by imposing a perfect reflection (mirror) boundary condition on a central region of the Gutsunaev-Manko spacetime, whose main properties we briefly review {in this section .}
elements.sent-2411-033: For simplicity, we will also use the name ``star'' for the compact object hereafter.
elements.sent-2411-034: The Gutsunaev-Manko solution is an axisymmetric stationary metric which can be derived from the Ernst equations in the Weyl gauge \cite{manko1989, manko1993 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: It is more conveniently expressed using the prolate spheroidal coordinates $(x,y,\varphi)$, which {are related to the usual cylindrical coordinates $\rho$ and $z$ by \begin{eqnarray}x = \frac{1}{2k}\left[\sqrt{\rho^2 + (z+k)^2} + \sqrt{\rho^2 + (z-k)^2}\right] , y = \frac{1}{2k}\left[ \sqrt{\rho^2 + (z+k)^2} - \sqrt{\rho^2 + (z-k)^2} \right] ,\end{eqnarray} where $k$ is a dimensional constant related to the mass of the central body.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The {ranges of the prolate coordinates $x$ and $y$, which are dimensionless by construction, are $x\ge 1$ and $-1\le y \le 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The inverse transformations are also useful \begin{eqnarray}\rho = k \sqrt{(x^2 - 1)(1 - y^2)} \, , z = kxy \, .\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-038: The Gutsunaev-Manko metric reads \begin{widetext \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM} \mathrm{d}s^2 = -\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1} \right)f^2 \mathrm{d}\tau^2 + \left(\frac{kg}{f}\right)^2 \left[ \frac{x+1}{x-1} {\mathrm{d}x^2} + \frac{(x+1)^2}{1-y^2}\mathrm{d}y^2 \right] + \frac{(x+1)^2(1-y^2)}{f^2}k^2\mathrm{d}\phi^2,\end{equation} \end{widetext where \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:f_deff} f = \frac{\left[x^2-y^2+\alpha^2(x^2-1)\right]^2 + 4\alpha^2x^2(1-y^2)} {\left[ x^2-y^2+\alpha^2(x-1)^2 \right]^2 - 4\alpha^2y^2(x^2-1)} ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:g_deff} g = \left\{\frac{ \left[x^2-y^2+\alpha^2(x^2-1) \right]^2 + 4\alpha^2x^2(1-y^2) } {(\alpha^2+1)^2(x^2-y^2)^2}\right\}^2,\end{equation} with $\alpha$ being a dimensionless parameter, whose interpretation will become clear later.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: For $\alpha=0$, we have $f=g=1$ and the Gutsunaev-Manko metric~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ) reduces to the Schwarzschild solution in prolate coordinates, with $x=1$ and $-1\le y \le 1$ representing the event horizon.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Also, the metric~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ), with the functions~(\ref{eq:MAS:f_deff ) and (\ref{eq:MAS:g_deff ), is asymptotically flat.}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In the limit $x\to \infty$, we have \begin{equation}\label{asympt} g_{\tau\tau}= -1 + \frac{2-6\alpha^2}{\alpha^2 + 1}x^{-1} + O(x^{-2}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-042: Taking into consideration that for $x\to \infty$ we have $r=kx$ in spherical coordinates, the asymptotic expansion (\ref{asympt ) implies that Eq.~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ) corresponds to a central object with total mass \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:GMmass} M = \frac{ 1 - 3 \alpha^2 }{1+ \alpha^2}k \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The vector potential associated with the metric~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ) is $A_\mu = (0,0,0,A_\phi)$, where \begin{eqnarray}A_\phi = \frac{4k\alpha^3}{1+\alpha^2}(1-y^2)\times \frac{ 2(1+ \alpha^2)x^3 + (1 - 3 \alpha^2)x^2 + y^2 + \alpha^2}{ ( x^2 - y^2 + \alpha^2 (x^2 - 1))^2 + 4 \alpha^2 x^2 (1-y^2) }\nonumber\label{Aphi},\end{eqnarray} {whose asymptotic expansion for $x\to \infty $ is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:Maglim} A_\phi = \frac{8k^2 \alpha^3}{(1 + \alpha^2)^2 } \frac{\sin^2\theta }{r} + O(x^{-2}),\end{equation} with the spherical polar angle expressed as as $y=\cos\theta$ for $x\to \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: From this asymptotic expansion, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:Maglim2} A_bA^b = g^{\phi\phi}A_\phi A_\phi = \mu^2 \frac{\sin^2\theta}{r^4},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAS:GMdipole} \mu = \frac{8 k^2 \alpha^3}{(1 + \alpha^2)^2} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: It is clear from (\ref{eq:MAS:Maglim2 ) that the vector potential~(\ref{Aphi ) corresponds to a central magnetic dipole with intensity given by (\ref{eq:MAS:GMdipole ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Notice that with $\alpha$ in the range $-\frac{1 {\sqrt{3 <\alpha < \frac{1 {\sqrt{3 }$ one can effectively describe all possibilities of mass $M$ and magnetization $\mu$ for the central object since \begin{equation}\label{fraction} \frac{8\alpha^3}{(1-3\alpha^2)^2}= \frac{\mu}{M^2} \, .\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The Gutsunaev-Manko metric is known to be plagued with curvature singularities.
elements.sent-2411-048: For {the sake of illustration, let us consider the determinant of the metric~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ) on $x=1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We have \begin{equation}\sqrt{|g_{ab}|} = \frac{4k^3}{\left(1+\alpha^2\right)^8} \left(1+\frac{4\alpha^2}{1-y^2}\right)^2 \, ,\end{equation} which clearly diverges at the poles $y = \pm 1$ for $\alpha\ne 0$, strongly suggesting the presence of a curvature singularity.
elements.sent-2411-050: In fact, the denominator of the function $f$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:MAS:f_deff ) has zeros near $x=1$ and $y = \pm 1$, indicating once more the existence of curvature singularities.}
elements.sent-2411-051: To avoid these problems, we will consider the Gutsunaev-Manko metric only for $x>x_{\mathrm{min }$, with $x_{\mathrm{min }$ large enough to hide the spacetime singularities.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We interpret the surface $x=x_{\mathrm{min }$ as the boundary of a magnetized prolate spheroidal body.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Our main goal here is to investigate the stability of the exterior region of the magnetized compact object introduced in the last section.
elements.sent-2411-054: For sake of simplicity, we will consider massless scalar perturbations, whose dynamics is {described by the standard {Klein-Gordon wave equation \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\sqrt{-g}}\partial_{a}\left( \sqrt{-g}g^{ab }\partial_{b}\Psi\right) =0 \, .\label{dleq}\end{equation} It is more convenient for our purposes here to introduce the dimensionless time variable $t = k^{-1}\tau$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: In terms of this new variable, the Klein-Gordon equation for the metric~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ) reads \begin{eqnarray}\label{wave} - \partial_t^2\Psi + \frac{(x-1)f^4}{(x+1)^3 g^2}\left[ \partial_x (x^2-1) \partial_x\Psi + \partial_y (1-y^2) \partial_y\Psi \phantom{\frac{1}{ 2 }}\right. \nonumber \quad\quad\quad\quad \quad\quad\quad\quad \quad +\left. \frac{g^2}{ 1-y^2 }\partial_\phi^2\Psi\right] = 0 \, .\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-056: This is our main equation, which will be studied with the total reflection boundary condition $\Psi=0$ on the compact body surface $x=x_{\mathrm{min }$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Since the metric~(\ref{eq:MAS:WeylGaugeGM ) is asymptotically flat, one expects on physical grounds that any perturbation on the exterior region of our compact body escapes toward infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, as we will see, the magnetization of the central body affects the {perturbative dynamics, making the relaxation faster.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Multiply Eq.~(\ref{eq:MP:ScalWave3 ) by $\bar Y^{m' _{\ell'}$, integrate with respect to $y$ and $\phi$, and invoke the orthogonality condition~(\ref{ortho ), leading to \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:ScalWave4} - \partial_t^2u_{\ell m} + \sum_{\ell'}A_{\ell}^{\ell' } \partial_{\tilde x}^2 u_{\ell' m } = \sum_{\ell'}B_{\ell}^{\ell'}u_{\ell' m } ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:Amatrix} A_{\ell}^{\ell' } = \frac{1}{k^2}\left\langle Y_\ell^m ,\frac{f^4}{g^2}Y_{\ell'}^{m} \right\rangle ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:Bmatrix} B_{\ell}^{\ell' } = \frac{x-1}{(x+1)^3} \left( A_\ell^{\ell'}V_{\mathrm{eff}} + \frac{m^2}{k^2} \left\langle Y_\ell^m ,\frac{g^2-1}{1-y^2} \frac{f^4}{g^2}Y_{\ell'}^{m} \right\rangle \right) \, ,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:VeffScal} V_{eff} = \ell(\ell + 1) + \frac{2}{x+1} \, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: It is worth noting that for $g$ as described in Eq.~(\ref{eq:MAS:g_deff ), the limit \begin{equation}\lim_{y \to \pm 1} \frac{g^2 - 1}{1 - y^2} = 1\end{equation} is well defined and, consequently, the integration performed in equations~(\ref{eq:MP:Bmatrix ) converges for every combination of $\ell$ and $\ell'$ in the external region.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Equations~(\ref{eq:MP:ScalWave4 ) form a set of coupled wave equations in the coordinates $(t,\tilde x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: They are the basis of our numerical time-domain analysis, which main results are described in the following subsection.
elements.sent-2411-063: Notice that, for the actual numerical calculations, one needs to truncate the sum in Eq. (\ref{eq:MP:ScalWave4 ) by introducing a cutoff $\ell_{\mathrm{max }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: As such, for a given a value of $m$, the parameter $\ell'$ takes integer values in the interval $[|m|, \ell_{\mathrm{max ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The matrices couple only terms of the same parity, which stems from the integration over the $y$-coordinate of an even function when $\ell$ and $\ell'$ have the same parity, and odd when they have different parities.
elements.sent-2411-066: The explicitly behavior of the matrices (\ref{eq:MP:Amatrix ) and (\ref{eq:MP:Bmatrix ) is displayed in Figures \ref{Amatrix and \ref{Bmatrix .}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig1a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig1b.pdf} \caption{Top: $A_{\ell}^{\ell}$ diagonal terms. Bottom: $A_{\ell}^{\ell'}$ off diagonal terms. Both present divergences at $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{sing}}\approx-9.07$ (dashed line) for $\alpha=0.057$.}\label{Amatrix}
elements.sent-2411-067: It is worth noticing that, provided $\ell_{\mathrm{max }$ is large enough, the QNM frequencies analysis is effectively independent of the employed cutoff, and this can be understood due to the tiny effects of the off-diagonal terms of the matrices $A^{\ell _{\ell}$ and $B^{\ell _{\ell}$, as long as $x_{\mathrm{min }$ is far enough from singularities.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Otherwise, the $\ell_{\mathrm{max }$ might be relevant, especially for higher values of multipole momenta.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In light of this, in all numerical calculations we ensures that $x_{\mathrm{min }$ is sufficiently distant from singularities.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig2a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig2b.pdf} \caption{Top: $B_{\ell}^{\ell'}$ diagonal terms. Bottom: $B_{\ell}^{\ell'}$ off diagonal terms. Just as previous matrices, This one presents divergences at $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{sing}}\approx-9.07$ (dashed line) for $\alpha=0.057$.}\label{Bmatrix}
elements.sent-2411-070: In order to solve the differential equation~(\ref{eq:MP:ScalWave4 ), we employ the same strategy used in \cite{molina , namely a finite-difference scheme with the spatial derivative calculated at fourth order and the time derivative at second order, see Figure \ref{fig:grade .}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: To avoid unnecessary clumsy notation, we will drop the $\ell$ and $m$ indices and denote the integration lattice position by a par of integers $i$ and $j$.
elements.sent-2411-072: At our proposed accuracy level, the second derivatives are \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:Sim:DiscreteDerivativeX} \partial_{\tilde x}^2u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{12 (\Delta \tilde x)^2} \left( -u_{i -2, j} + 16 u_{i -1, j} - 30 u_{i , j} \right.\nonumber \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad +\left. 16 u_{i +1, j} - u_{i +2, j} \right)\end{eqnarray} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:Sim:DiscreteDerivativeT} \partial_t^2 u_{i , j} = \frac{1}{\Delta t^2} \left(u_{i , j+1} - 2 u_{i , j} - u_{i , j-1} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Sim:DiscreteDerivativeX} \partial_{\tilde x}^2u_{i,j} = \frac{1}{12 (\Delta \tilde x)^2} \left( -u_{i -2, j} + 16 u_{i -1, j} - 30 u_{i , j} \right.\nonumber \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad +\left. 16 u_{i +1, j} - u_{i +2, j} \right)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Sim:DiscreteDerivativeT} \partial_t^2 u_{i , j} = \frac{1}{\Delta t^2} \left(u_{i , j+1} - 2 u_{i , j} - u_{i , j-1} \right).\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{fig3.pdf} \caption{Two-dimensional fourth/second finite-difference discretization, see \cite{molina} for further details. Our numerical results were performed using the python code finite-difference-GM.py which is publicly available at \cite{finiteElementsGM}. } \label{fig:grade}
elements.sent-2411-073: The parameter space of our model is spanned $\alpha$ (constant related to the magnetic field), $x_{\mathrm{min }$ (position of the mirror), $\ell$ (multipole number of the scalar field) and $n$ (overtone number of the scalar field).}
elements.sent-2411-074: This parameter space has been thoroughly explored.
elements.sent-2411-075: For sake of illustration, we present in Table \ref{tab:Combined_Frequencies_NoGaps the results corresponding to three different positions of the mirror: $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min =-15, -10,$ and $-5$, and a cutoff $\ell_{\mathrm{max =5$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: For each of these $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min }$ values, the range of $\alpha$ was chosen in equal intervals from zero up to the convergence limit, respectively, $\alpha_{\mathrm{max = 0.01,\, 0.03,$ and $ 0.11$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-077: For all considered cases we have found usual decaying quasinormal oscillations, for all tested values of $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Moreover, we found out that the stronger the magnetic dipole intensity, the larger is the imaginary part of the frequency of the QNM.
elements.sent-2411-079: The dependence of both the real and imaginary parts of the quasinormal frequencies on $\alpha$ can be well approximated by the quadratic expression $1+b\alpha^2$ with $b>0$, see Figure~\ref{fig:x-freq .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Such a quadratic behavior is not surprising, at least for small $\alpha$, since both metric functions $f$ and $g$ depend explicitly only on the square of $\alpha$.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig4a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig4b.pdf} \caption{ Dependence of the complex fundamental quasinormal frequencies $\omega$ on the parameter $\alpha$. The blue solid line is the variation expected from the dependence of $M^{-1}$ on $\alpha$, namely $\frac{1+\alpha^2}{1-3\alpha^2}$, see (\ref{eq:MAS:GMmass}). The (red) circles, (cyan) squares, and (green) stars are, respectively, the quasinormal frequencies for $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{min}}=-5, -10,$ and $-15$. The dashed lines are the quadratic fit $1+b\alpha^2$. The values of $b$ are, respectively, $b \approx 4.9,\, 5.4, $ and $5.4$ (Top), and $9.7,\, 13.7, $ and $14.8$ (Bottom).} \label{fig:x-freq}
elements.sent-2411-081: It is important to stress that the obtained quadratic dependence on $\alpha$ is different from that one would initially expected from the dependence of the mass $M$ of the star on $\alpha$ given by (\ref{eq:MAS:GMmass ), and this is indeed one of our main findings.}
elements.sent-2411-082: In analogy with the usual Schwarzschild and other black hole examples, one might expect the quasinormal frequencies to be proportional to $M^{-1}$ and, hence, proportional to $1+4\alpha^2$ for small $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-083: Nevertheless, from our fits, we have that the $b$ coefficient for the imaginary part of the frequencies is always greater than the real part, meaning that the ``stabilization'' phenomena of the quasinormal modes is not solely due the mass variations, but it indeed increases with the magnetization of the star, see Figure~\ref{fig5 .}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig5.pdf} \caption{Path in the complex plane of the curve given by $\omega(\alpha)/\omega(0)$. The (red) circles, (cyan) squares, and (green) stars correspond, respectively, to $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{min}}=-5, -10,$ and $-15$. Notice the clear deviation from the case with a simple dependence of the type $\omega(\alpha)\propto M^{-1}(\alpha)$, which would correspond to the dashed horizontal lines. This a clear indication that the QNM spectrum does depend directly on the magnetization of the compact body, and not only indirectly through the mass dependence on $\alpha$. } \label{fig5}
elements.sent-2411-084: It is worth noticing that the effect of $\alpha$, and hence of the magnetic field, on the perturbations was typically small, on the order of a hundredth to a few tenths of the value of the frequency, and thus much smaller than the effect of the size of the star.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig6.pdf} \caption{The power-law tail $t^{-r}$ as function of $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{min}}$ and $\alpha$. The data is well fitted by the quadratic expression $r_0(1+a\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{min}})(1+b\alpha^2)$, see the body text for further details. The main graphics depicts the values of $r(\alpha)$ for $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{min}} = -3, -3, -1, 0,$ and $1$. All data points are clustered up to the graphics resolution. The dashed line is the quadratic dependence on $\alpha$. The inset shows the linear dependence of $r(0)$ on $\tilde{x}_{\mathrm{min}}$.} \label{fig:Tail}
elements.sent-2411-085: Even for the maximum excursion of $\alpha$ studied in these runs, the real frequency differed from the non-magnetized case by 5\%, while the imaginary frequency varied by about 15\%.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, a variation of the mirror from $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min = -10$ to $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min = -5$, corresponding to a variation of 2\% in the radius of the star, would produce a variation of a tenfold factor for the imaginary frequency and of about 30\% for the real frequency.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For all considered cases, the quasinormal oscillations were followed by a final power-law tail $\propto t^{-r}$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Such tails also have their behavior dependent on the magnetization and the size of the star.
elements.sent-2411-089: Figure \ref{fig:Tail depicts the results for $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min }\in [-3,1]$ and the magnetization parameter $\alpha\in [0,2]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: These parameters have antagonistic effects on the power-law tail.
elements.sent-2411-091: The dependence of the power-law exponent $r$ on $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min }$ and $\alpha$ is well fitted by the quadratic expression $r_0(1+a\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min )(1+b\alpha^2)$, with $a\approx 7\times 10^{-4}$, $b\approx -3.3\times 10^{-2}$, and $r_0\approx 5.97$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: As one can see, the power-law exponent $r$ increases linearly with the size of the star, meaning that the tail decays faster, and, conversely, for fixed $\tilde{x _{\mathrm{min }$ , it decreases quadratically, implying that the tail decays at a slower pace.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Nevertheless, the values of $r$ accumulate around $r=6$, the expected power-law tail of a Schwarzschild Black Hole perturbed by static initial conditions.

elements.sent-2411-100: By using $u_{\ell\omega = e^{i\omega t v_{\ell\omega}$ in the decomposition (\ref{deco ), we have the following ordinary differential equation \begin{eqnarray}\label{xradialEq1} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}v }{\mathrm{d}x^{2}}+\frac{2}{x^{2}-1}\frac {\mathrm{d}v }{\mathrm{d}x} -\frac{1}{x-1}\left[ \frac{\ell \left( \ell+1\right) }{x+1}-\frac{\left( x+1\right) ^{2} \omega^{2}}{x-1}+\frac {2}{\left( x+1\right) ^{2}}\right] v =0 \, ,\nonumber\end{eqnarray} where, for simplicity, we have dropped the $\ell \omega$ indices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Equation (\ref{xradialEq1 ) can be extended to the entire complex plane and recast into a confluent Heun differential equation \cite{Heun \begin{equation}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}v}{\mathrm{d}z^{2}}+\left( \epsilon+\frac{\beta+1}{z} +\frac{\gamma+1}{z-1}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}z}+\left( \frac{\mu}{z}+\frac{\nu}{z-1}\right) v=0 ,\label{eqCHeun}\end{equation} with \begin{eqnarray}\mu = \frac{1}{2}(\epsilon -\beta -\gamma + \epsilon\beta -\beta\gamma) -\eta , \nu = \frac{1}{2}(\epsilon +\beta +\gamma + \epsilon\gamma +\beta\gamma) +\eta .\end{eqnarray}.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The confluent Heun differential equation appears frequently in QNM studies, see for instance \cite{Heun, fiziev2006exact,Horta_su_2018,bruno1,bruno2 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Their solutions have two regular singular points ($z=0,1$) and one irregular point located at infinity.
elements.sent-2411-104: Local solutions of Eq.~(\ref{eqCHeun ) around the regular singular points are written in terms of the Heun confluent function, often denoted by $\mathrm{HeunC}\left( \epsilon,\beta,\gamma,\delta ,\eta;z\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Thus, the general solution $U_{X (z)$ around the point $X=0$ or $X=\infty$ can be written as \begin{equation}U_X(z) = C^{+}_{X}H^{+}_{X}\left( z\right)+C^{-}_{X}H^{-}_{X}\left( z\right) ,\end{equation} where $C^{\pm _X$ are constants.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Let us return to the original problem of solving Eq.~(\ref{xradialEq1 ) with the Dirichlet boundary condition on the surface of the star, \begin{equation}v(x_{\mathrm{min}})=0 .\label{DBC}\end{equation} It is convenient here to introduce the the following ansatz \begin{equation}v \left( z\right) =(1-z)^{2i\omega}\left( z+1\right)e^{i\omega z} w .\label{ansatz}\end{equation} One can see that the function $w$ also satisfies a confluent Heun differential equation.}
elements.sent-2411-108: In terms of this ansatz, the solution of the confluent Heun equation (\ref{eqCHeun ) obeying the Dirichlet boundary condition (\ref{DBC ) reads \begin{widetext \begin{equation}\label{sol1} v (z) = C_{1}(1-z)^{2ik\omega} (z+1) e^{ik\omega z}\times \Bigg[HeunC\left(\epsilon,\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-z}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{1-x_{min}}{1-z}\right)^{4ik\omega}\!\!\! \frac{HeunC\left(\epsilon,\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-x_{min}}{2}\right)}{HeunC\left(\epsilon,-\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-x_{min}}{2}\right)} HeunC\left(\epsilon,-\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-z}{2}\right)\Bigg] ,\nonumber\end{equation} \end{widetext where \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\epsilon =-4i\omega, \quad \beta =4i\omega, \quad \gamma =0, \delta =-8\omega^{2}, \quad \eta =8\omega^{2}- \ell \left( \ell+1\right).\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: To accomplish this, we must ensure that there are no ingoing modes coming from infinity.
elements.sent-2411-110: The solution can be also written in terms of asymptotic series at infinity, \begin{equation}v (z)=C_{\infty}^{+}U^{+}_{\infty}(z)+C_{\infty}^{-}U^{-}_{\infty}(z),\label{infinitosolution}\end{equation} where, in accordance with our ansatz (\ref{ansatz ), \begin{equation}U_\infty^{\pm}(z)=(1-z)^{2i\omega}(z+1)e^{i\omega z}H_\infty^{\pm}(z) .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-111: By exploiting the fact that the solution approaches zero as $z\to \infty$, it is possible to constraint the constants $ C^{\pm _{\infty $ as \begin{equation}\left | C_{\infty}^{-} \right |= \left | C_{\infty}^{+} \right |\lim_{z\to \infty}\left|\frac{U^{+}_{\infty}(z)}{U^{-}_{\infty}(z)}\right|, \label{C-}\end{equation} from where we have that $C_{\infty ^{- = 0$ for \begin{equation}\lim_{z\to \infty}\left|U^{-}_{\infty}(z)\right| = \infty . \label{condition}\end{equation} As noted in \cite{fiziev2006exact , condition~(\ref{condition ) is satisfied in the lower half of the complex plane, \begin{equation}\mathrm{\arg}\left( z\right) +\mathrm{\arg}\left(\omega\right) \in\left( -\pi,0\right) .\end{equation}.}}}}}}

\begin{equation}\arg\left( z\right) =-\frac{\pi}{2}-\arg\left(\omega\right) }}}\Bigg| \left( 1-z\right) ^{4i\omega}\frac {HeunC\left( \epsilon,\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-z}{2}\right) }{HeunC\left( \epsilon,-\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-z}{2}\right) } -\left( 1-x_{min}\right) ^{4i\omega}\frac{HeunC\left( \epsilon,\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-x_{min}}{2}\right) }{HeunC\left( \epsilon,-\beta,0,\delta,\eta;\frac{1-x_{min}}{2}\right) }\Bigg |=0 \, . \label{eqtransc}\end{equation} \end{widetext Eq.~(\ref{eqtransc ) is valid only for discrete values of $\omega_n$, with the first mode, corresponding to $n=0$, referred to as the fundamental mode.}}}

elements.sent-2411-114: We have opted to recast the complex equation in a system of two real equations in two real variables, and use a standard Newton method to numerically determine the first modes, whose values are shown in Table \ref{tab:Modes_Frequencies .}

elements.sent-2411-116: We have shown that the spheroidal magnetized compact body described by a simple modification of the Gutsunaev-Manko is well-defined and stable.
elements.sent-2411-117: Moreover, we have observed that the magnetic field has a slightly stabilizing effect on QNM spectrum, {\it i.e. , greater magnetization leads to faster decay of the modes.}
elements.sent-2411-118: An opposite effect was noted in the tails regime.
elements.sent-2411-119: For any practical purpose, we must be able to express the mass $M$ and the magnetic moment $\mu$ of our compact object in terms of usual astrophysical units.
elements.sent-2411-120: This can be easily done by recasting the $G_N$ and $c$ factors in (\ref{eq:MAS:GMmass ) and (\ref{eq:MAS:GMdipole ).}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Recalling that $G_NM_\odot /c^2 \approx 1.48$ km, we have \begin{equation}M =\frac{1-3\alpha^2}{1+\alpha^2}\left(\frac{k}{1.48 \,{\rm km}}\right)M_\odot,\label{physicalMass}\end{equation} with, of course, $k$ given in km.
elements.sent-2411-122: For the magnetic moment $\mu$, the situation is a little bit more intricate.
elements.sent-2411-123: From (\ref{eq:MAS:Maglim2 ), we have that the magnitude of the magnetic field in our solution is such that $|B|\propto \mu/r^3$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Hence, to accommodate the typical magnetic field ($10^{13}$ to $10^{15}$ G) of a magnetar with superficial radius of $10$ km, the magnetic moment $\mu$ must be of the order $10^{16}$ to $10^{18 \,{\rm G}\,{\rm km ^3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Recalling that in Gaussian units the magnetic dipole moment $\mu$ is expressed in ${\rm erg / {\rm G = {\rm cm ^\frac{5 {2 {\rm g ^\frac{1 {2 {\rm s ^{-1}$, we have that $\sqrt{G}\mu/{c^2}$ has units ${\rm cm ^2$ and the units of (\ref{eq:MAS:GMdipole ) can be restored straightforwardly. \begin{equation}\mu =\frac{8\alpha^3}{\left(1+\alpha^2\right)^2}\frac{c^2k^2}{\sqrt{G_N }} =\frac{8\alpha^3}{\left(1+\alpha^2\right)^2} \left(\frac{k}{1.48 \,{\rm km}}\right)^2 \frac{G_N^\frac{3}{2}M^2_\odot}{c^2} ,\label{physicaldipole}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\frac{G_N^\frac{3}{2}M^2_\odot}{c^2} \approx 7.6\times 10^{19} \,{\rm G}\,{\rm km}^3 .\label{physicaldipole1}\end{equation} One can see that our model can describe the typical magnetic field observed in magnetars for small values of $\alpha$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Although we have focused only on fundamental frequencies for the perfect reflection (mirror) model, the excited modes can also be extracted using numerical methods such as those ones discussed in \cite{London_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Finally, the analysis conducted in this work is restricted to uncharged scalar perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-128: Therefore, its straightforward extension would be to introduce other fields with distinct spin and charges.
elements.sent-2411-129: Electrically charged fields, in particular, would be especially interesting since they could interact electromagnetically with the magnetic moment present in our model based on the Gutsunaev-Manko solution.

elements.sent-2411-133: Such a solution is certainly of astrophysical interest since it provides a simple yet viable description of the exterior region of { non-rotating magnetized bodies.}
elements.sent-2411-134: The closest magnetized astrophysical body to the Earth is the Sun.
elements.sent-2411-135: Its field strength is rather feeble by astrophysical standards, approximately $1$ G on its surface, only twice the Earth's magnetic field strength, but its dipole component gives origin to the heliospheric magnetic field which is responsible for many relevant phenomena in the solar system \cite{TheSun .}
elements.sent-2411-136: Nevertheless, it is well known that the Sun has strong local perturbations on its magnetic field, reaching strengths of the order of $10^3$ G on some spots.
elements.sent-2411-137: Moreover, the Sun dipole is known to exhibit a regular dynamics, changing its polarity in approximate 11-year periods.
elements.sent-2411-138: Although our model might be useful to describe fast phenomena -- compared with the dipole changes period -- of low magnetization stars like the Sun, we think it is particularly more interesting for the study of magnetars, {\it i.e. , highly magnetized neutron stars, typically with low angular momentum \cite{Kaspi_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-139: The intense magnetic fields (of the order of $10^{13}$ to $10^{15}$ G) are prone to produce high-energy electromagnetic radiation phenomena {such as bursts and flares of X-rays, gamma-rays, and perhaps even Fast Radio Bursts (FRB) \cite{Kirsten_2020, zhang2020physical .}}
elements.sent-2411-140: This possibility has gained interest due to the recently reported detection \cite{ChimeFrb_1 of a repeating FRB from the magnetar SGR 1935+2154, which lies on the outskirts of our own Milky Way.}
elements.sent-2411-141: On physical grounds, one expects that such totally-reflected QNMs are stable, and we found that they indeed are.
elements.sent-2411-142: Multiply Eq.~(\ref{eq:MP:ScalWave3 ) by $\bar Y^{m' _{\ell'}$, integrate with respect to $y$ and $\phi$, and invoke the orthogonality condition~(\ref{ortho ), leading to \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:ScalWave4} - \partial_t^2u_{\ell m} + \sum_{\ell'}A_{\ell}^{\ell' } \partial_{\tilde x}^2 u_{\ell' m } = \sum_{\ell'}B_{\ell}^{\ell'}u_{\ell' m } ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:Amatrix} A_{\ell}^{\ell' } = \left\langle Y_\ell^m ,\frac{f^4}{g^2}Y_{\ell'}^{m} \right\rangle ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:Bmatrix} B_{\ell}^{\ell' } = \frac{x-1}{(x+1)^3} \left( A_\ell^{\ell'}V_{\mathrm{eff}} + {m^2} \left\langle Y_\ell^m ,\frac{g^2-1}{1-y^2} \frac{f^4}{g^2}Y_{\ell'}^{m} \right\rangle \right) \, ,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:MP:VeffScal} V_{eff} = \ell(\ell + 1) + \frac{2}{x+1} .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: In such a limit, we have $f=g=1$ and, consequently, $A_{\ell ^{\ell' = \delta_{\ell ^{\ell' }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: The $B_{\ell ^{\ell' }$ matrix (\ref{eq:MP:Bmatrix ) is diagonal in this case and equation (\ref{eq:MP:ScalWave4 ) will read simply \begin{equation}- \partial_t^2u_{\ell m} + \partial_{\tilde x}^2 u_{\ell m } = \tilde{V}_{\rm eff} u_{\ell m },\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\tilde V_{eff} = \frac{x-1}{(x+1)^3}\left[ \ell(\ell + 1) + \frac{2}{x+1} \right] ,\end{equation} which is the usual effective potential for scalar (spin $0$) fields in the Schwarzschild case, as one can see by taking the limit of large $ x \approx r$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-045: \begin{equation}- \partial_t^2u_{\ell m} + \partial_{\tilde x}^2 u_{\ell m } = \tilde{V}_{\rm eff} u_{\ell m },\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-145: It is quite natural to expect that for perturbations with other spins $s$, we end up with the usual effective potential of the type \begin{equation}\tilde V_{eff} \approx \frac{ \ell(\ell + 1)}{r^2} + \frac{2(1-s^2)}{r^3} ,\end{equation} for large $r$, but the expression for the $B_{\ell ^{\ell' }$ matrix (\ref{eq:MP:Bmatrix ) are much more intricate for $s\ne 0$, rendering the analysis of other perturbation types in prolate coordinates a quite laborious task.}}
